posts.title: AI agents hit the boring phase
posts.hook: The most exciting thing happening with AI agents right now is how boring they're getting.
posts.body:
The most exciting thing happening with AI agents right now is how boring they're getting.

A year ago, every demo was a chatbot doing something flashy. Today the agents actually shipping in production are doing stuff like... updating CRM fields. Routing leads. Enriching contact records.

Nobody's posting viral demos of those.

But here's the pattern I keep seeing across teams that actually deploy agents vs. teams that keep prototyping:

1. They pick one workflow that a human currently does manually
2. They don't try to make the agent "smart." They make it reliable
3. They measure it against the human baseline, not against perfection

The teams still stuck in prototype mode? They're building general-purpose agents that can "handle anything." Which means they handle nothing well enough to trust.

I run agents in production for lead enrichment, content scheduling, and data sync. None of them are impressive in a demo. All of them save me 3-4 hours a day.

The gap between agent demos and agent deployments isn't technical. It's scope discipline.

Small, boring, reliable. That's the formula.
posts.cta: What's the most boring agent you've actually shipped to production?

remixes.label: graveyard-of-demos
remixes.hook: The AI agent graveyard is full of impressive demos.
remixes.body:
The AI agent graveyard is full of impressive demos.

I've watched dozens of teams build agents that could summarize earnings calls, generate marketing copy, or have multi-turn conversations about complex topics. Beautiful demos. Standing ovations in all-hands meetings.

Then they hit production and died within weeks.

The agents that actually survived? They do one narrow thing. They update a field in your CRM when a deal stage changes. They route inbound leads based on company size. They enrich a contact record with firmographic data so your SDR doesn't have to tab between five tools.

Nobody claps for that in a demo. But those agents run every single day without breaking.

The pattern is consistent. The teams that start with "what manual task costs us the most time" ship agents that stick. The teams that start with "what would look cool with AI" ship demos that rot.

I stopped being impressed by agent capabilities months ago. Now I just ask one question. Is it still running? If an agent is still doing its job three months after launch with minimal babysitting, that's the real flex. Everything else is a science fair project.
remixes.cta: What's still running in your stack three months after you shipped it?

remixes.label: tactical-breakdown
remixes.hook: The three production agents that actually moved our pipeline numbers weren't built by engineers.
remixes.body:
The three production agents that actually moved our pipeline numbers weren't built by engineers.

They were built by ops people who were tired of doing the same thing 40 times a day.

Agent one. A lead routing agent that reads inbound form submissions, checks company size against our ICP criteria in Attio, and assigns the lead to the right rep. Took two days to build. Replaced a process that used to take 20 minutes per lead.

Agent two. A CRM enrichment agent that watches for new contacts, hits a few data APIs, and fills in missing fields like industry, headcount, and tech stack. Runs on a cron. Nobody thinks about it.

Agent three. A deal hygiene agent that flags stale opportunities where the last activity was more than 14 days ago. Sends a Slack nudge to the rep. Simple. Annoying. Effective.

None of these required a PhD. None of them use RAG or vector databases or multi-agent orchestration. They just connect APIs and apply basic logic.

The boring middle of AI agents is where the actual ROI lives. The teams winning right now figured that out early.
remixes.cta: Drop your most unglamorous agent below. The more boring, the more I want to hear about it.

posts.title: GTM engineering isn't a title
posts.hook: GTM engineering isn't a job title. It's what happens when an SDR learns to code.
posts.body:
GTM engineering isn't a job title. It's what happens when an SDR learns to code.

I watch teams hire "GTM engineers" who've never manually built a buying committee. Never sent 200 cold emails in a day. Never felt the pain of updating Salesforce contact roles by hand.

Then they wonder why the automations don't work.

The best GTM engineers I know all share the same arc. They did the manual work first. They felt every friction point. Then they automated the specific things that hurt.

That's different from a developer who reads a RevOps blog and builds a pipeline in Clay. The developer builds what looks logical. The former SDR builds what actually converts.

Specific example. When I built my first outbound enrichment pipeline, I didn't start with a 47-step Clay table. I started with the three fields I always wished I had when I was prospecting manually:

➡️ Tech stack confirmation (not guessed from job posts)
➡️ Recent funding or hiring signals
➡️ Whether they had an existing vendor in my category

Three columns. That's it. Everything else came later, driven by real pipeline feedback.

The reps who grind first and automate second build better systems than engineers who've never carried a quota.
posts.cta: Former SDRs turned builders... what was the first thing you automated?

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building a GTM engineering function from scratch, integrating outbound automation tools like Apollo and Clay to personalize at scale. Emphasizes aligning sales ops with revenue operations for predictable growth. Shares a framework for automating 80% of outbound while maintaining human touch.

remixes.label: reverse-career-path
remixes.hook: The best engineers I've hired for GTM automation were the worst at writing clean code.
remixes.body:
The best engineers I've hired for GTM automation were the worst at writing clean code.

They wrote ugly scripts. Variable names that made senior devs cry. Zero tests. But every single automation they built actually worked. Because they understood the problem from the inside.

They'd spent years in the trenches. Manually qualifying leads. Copy-pasting data between tabs. Building pivot tables in spreadsheets to figure out which accounts to prioritize.

When they finally learned enough Python or JavaScript to be dangerous, they didn't build elegant systems. They built solutions to problems that had been burning them for years.

That's the difference. A traditional engineer looks at a GTM workflow and sees a systems design problem. A former SDR turned builder looks at the same workflow and sees 47 specific pain points they personally experienced.

The code quality comes later. The domain knowledge comes first.

I'd take a scrappy former BDR who taught themselves to code over a senior engineer who's never had to hit quota. Every time. The engineer can learn sales operations. But the intuition for what actually matters in a pipeline? That only comes from doing the work.
remixes.cta: What skill from your non-technical career turned out to be your biggest advantage as a builder?

remixes.label: hiring-anti-pattern
remixes.hook: Stop hiring GTM engineers who've never done outbound.
remixes.body:
Stop hiring GTM engineers who've never done outbound.

I see this mistake constantly. A company decides they need to automate their go-to-market motion. They post a job for a "GTM engineer." Requirements include Python, SQL, API integrations, maybe some data engineering experience.

What's missing? Any mention of actually doing the work they're automating.

So they hire someone who can build beautiful data pipelines. Elegant API integrations. Clean code. And the automations don't work. Not because the code is bad. Because the person who built them has never felt the pain of manual prospecting.

They automate the wrong steps. They optimize for engineering elegance instead of rep efficiency. They build dashboards nobody looks at because they've never sat in a pipeline review meeting.

The fix is simple. Pair your engineer with your best SDR for two weeks. Not to shadow. To actually do the job. Make them send cold emails. Make them update Salesforce by hand. Make them build a target account list from scratch.

After two weeks of that, they'll know exactly what to automate. And more importantly, what not to.
remixes.cta: Hiring managers: do you require GTM experience for your GTM engineering roles? Why or why not?

posts.title: GPU discourse is missing the point
posts.hook: Everyone's talking about GPU clusters and distributed training. Meanwhile, most companies can't even keep their CRM data clean.
posts.body:
Everyone's talking about GPU clusters and distributed training. Meanwhile, most companies can't even keep their CRM data clean.

The AI infrastructure conversation has this weird split. On one side, teams building foundation models need to think about GPU utilization and distributed compute. On the other side, 99% of companies just need to connect their existing tools and make the data flow.

I'm in that 99%.

My "AI infrastructure" is:

✅ A SQLite database that tracks initiatives
✅ Python scripts that run on cron
✅ Claude API calls that cost $3-8/day
✅ A Mac Mini that never sleeps

No Kubernetes. No vector databases. No orchestration layer. No ML ops platform.

And it handles lead enrichment, content generation, competitive monitoring, and CRM updates autonomously.

The infrastructure discourse is exciting but it creates this perception that you need serious compute to do serious AI work. You don't. Most business problems don't need a fine-tuned model. They need an API call, a good prompt, and a cron job.

The most underrated AI infrastructure is a $600 machine running scripts overnight.
posts.cta: What's your actual AI infrastructure look like? Not what you wish it looked like. What's running today.

inspired_by.author: Andrej Karpathy
inspired_by.summary: Explores advancements in AI infrastructure for training large models, focusing on efficient GPU utilization and distributed computing challenges. Details custom tools developed for developer workflows in AI research. Predicts shifts in how devs build with next-gen models.

remixes.label: infrastructure-spectrum
remixes.hook: Your company doesn't have an AI infrastructure problem. It has a plumbing problem.
remixes.body:
Your company doesn't have an AI infrastructure problem. It has a plumbing problem.

I keep having the same conversation with ops leaders. They come in talking about AI strategy. LLM selection. Model fine-tuning. RAG pipelines. Vector databases.

Then I ask, "Can you pull a clean list of all accounts in your pipeline with their current stage and last activity date?"

Silence.

The gap between AI discourse and AI reality is enormous. Twitter is debating GPU allocation strategies. Meanwhile, most B2B companies have three different definitions of "qualified lead" across their tools.

Your CRM has duplicates. Your marketing automation has contacts that don't exist in your CRM. Your CS tool has a different company name format than everything else. And someone's running critical workflows off a Google Sheet that only they understand.

No amount of AI sophistication fixes that. You can build the most elegant agent in the world, and it will produce garbage if the data flowing through it is garbage.

The unsexy truth is that most companies need to spend six months on data hygiene before they're ready for anything interesting with AI. Clean your pipes first. The fancy stuff comes after.

I've seen more ROI from a weekend of CRM cleanup than from six-figure AI platform investments.
remixes.cta: Be honest. What percentage of your CRM data would you trust an AI agent to act on without human review?

remixes.label: two-worlds-thesis
remixes.hook: There are two AI conversations happening right now, and they have almost nothing to do with each other.
remixes.body:
There are two AI conversations happening right now, and they have almost nothing to do with each other.

Conversation one happens on AI Twitter. GPU clusters. Distributed training. Scaling laws. Mixture of experts. Frontier model capabilities. This is fascinating and important for maybe 200 companies on the planet.

Conversation two happens in Slack threads at normal companies. "Why did that lead get routed to the wrong rep again?" "Can someone update the enrichment workflow? It's been broken for two weeks." "Does anyone know why our Salesforce data doesn't match HubSpot?"

Nearly every company is living in conversation two. But the content they consume is all about conversation one.

This creates a weird distortion. Teams feel behind because they're not doing RAG and vector search. But they haven't solved basic data flow between their CRM and marketing tools yet.

The actual AI infrastructure stack for 99% of companies is much simpler than the discourse suggests. An API connector here. A scheduled enrichment job there. Maybe a Claude call to classify or summarize something. All running on a single server or even a laptop.

That's not a sign of being unsophisticated. That's the appropriate level of infrastructure for the problem set.
remixes.cta: Which conversation is your company actually in? The GPU one or the "why is our CRM broken" one?

The AI infrastructure conversation has this weird split. On one side, teams building foundation models need to think about GPU utilization and distributed compute. On the other side, 99% of companies just need to connect their existing tools and make the data flow.

Here's what I'd recommend if you're figuring out your AI infrastructure. Ignore the frontier model discourse entirely. It's irrelevant to your situation.

Instead, audit three things. First, can you get a clean, deduplicated list of your target accounts from your CRM in under five minutes? If not, start there. Second, do your tools share a consistent data model? Same field names, same company identifiers, same lifecycle stages. Third, when data changes in one system, does it propagate to the others automatically?

If you answered no to any of those, you're not ready for advanced AI. You're ready for plumbing work. API connections between your core tools. Data normalization scripts. Deduplication routines.

This isn't exciting. But the companies I've seen get real value from AI agents all had this foundation in place first. The agent layer is only as good as the data layer beneath it. Fix the pipes before you build the penthouse.

Your actual AI infrastructure problem is embarrassingly simple. Your CRM has duplicate records. Your marketing data doesn't sync to your sales tools. Someone critical to your operations runs things through a Google Sheet with conditional formatting that only they understand.

And it works. Because the hard part was never compute. It was getting clean data flowing between tools.

The AI infrastructure industry has a vested interest in making you think you need more than you do. You probably don't.

posts.title: Solo builder math changed
posts.hook: Solo developers used to compete on speed. Now they compete on leverage.
posts.body:
Solo developers used to compete on speed. Now they compete on leverage.

The Pieter Levels playbook from 2019 was: ship fast, ship often, stay lean. That still works. But something shifted.

The solo builders winning now aren't just fast. They're running operations that would have required 3-5 people two years ago. Not because they work more hours. Because AI agents handle the repetitive middle.

I run a content operation across three websites, a LinkedIn presence, Reddit engagement, and outbound GTM. By myself. On one machine.

The breakdown:

🔧 Content research and drafting... AI-assisted, human-edited
🔧 SEO monitoring and keyword tracking... automated cron
🔧 Lead enrichment pipeline... Clay + scripts
🔧 Social engagement... AI-scouted, human-approved
🔧 Deployment and builds... CI/CD, no manual steps

None of these are fully autonomous. Every piece has a human checkpoint. But the 80% of work that's research, formatting, data entry, and scheduling? That's handled.

The old solo builder ceiling was "how many hours can you work." The new ceiling is "how many reliable automations can you maintain."

Different constraint. Different strategies. Much higher output ceiling.
posts.cta: Solo builders: what's the one automation that 10x'd your output?

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shares his journey as a solo developer shipping multiple products like Nomad List and Remote OK without VC funding. He emphasizes bootstrapping, iterating quickly based on user feedback, and the freedom of building in public. His post details revenue milestones and lessons from launching 12 startups in 12 months.

remixes.label: leverage-audit
remixes.hook: I tracked every hour I worked last month. 60% of my output was produced by agents, not me.
remixes.body:
I tracked every hour I worked last month. 60% of my output was produced by agents, not me.

Not in a vague "AI helped me brainstorm" way. In a literal, measurable way. Content drafted. Leads enriched. Data synced. Reports generated. Code committed.

I worked roughly 40 hours per week. But the output volume was equivalent to what a small team would produce. The difference is automated workflows running around the clock.

Here's what my daily stack actually looks like. A cron job runs at midnight that generates a blog post, enriches new leads, syncs CRM data, and updates reporting dashboards. Another set of agents handles content distribution throughout the day. A third handles inbound lead routing and qualification.

I designed those systems. I review their output. I step in when something breaks. But the repetitive execution? That's not me anymore.

This isn't about working less. I still work full days. But the nature of the work shifted completely. Less data entry, more strategy. Less copy-pasting between tools, more building new systems. Less maintenance, more creation.

The old solo builder advantage was speed. Ship faster than anyone else. The new advantage is leverage. Build systems that multiply your output while you sleep.
remixes.cta: If you tracked your work for a week, what percentage is still manual that could be automated?

remixes.label: anti-hustle-reframe
remixes.hook: The "work harder" era of solo building is over. The "build smarter" era just started.
remixes.body:
The "work harder" era of solo building is over. The "build smarter" era just started.

For years, the indie hacker playbook was simple. Outwork everyone. Ship at 2am. Push 7 days a week. Burn out, recover, repeat.

That playbook had a ceiling. One person, no matter how driven, maxes out at maybe 60-70 productive hours per week. And most of those hours were spent on repetitive tasks that didn't require creativity or judgment.

The new playbook looks different. Build once, run forever. A solo builder in 2026 spends their time designing systems, not executing tasks. The execution is automated.

Look at what's actually possible now. You can have agents monitoring your pipeline, enriching leads, drafting outreach, publishing content, and updating your CRM. All running on crons while you focus on the work that actually requires a human brain.

The ceiling moved. It's not about how many hours you can work. It's about how many autonomous loops you can set up and maintain.

I know solo builders shipping more than some 10-person teams. Not because they're superhuman. Because they spent time building the machine instead of being the machine.

The leverage gap between solo builders who've figured this out and those who haven't is getting wider every month.

posts.title: RevOps unification is a myth
posts.hook:
"Unify sales ops, marketing ops, and CS ops into one RevOps team."

Sounds great. Almost never works the way people describe it.
posts.body:
"Unify sales ops, marketing ops, and CS ops into one RevOps team."

Sounds great. Almost never works the way people describe it.

The RevOps pitch is clean. One team, one data model, one source of truth. Alignment across the funnel.

The reality is three people who used to own their domains now report to someone who understands maybe one of those domains deeply.

What actually works better in my experience:

Keep domain expertise separate. Unify the data layer.

Your marketing ops person should still own attribution and campaign logic. Your sales ops person should still own pipeline mechanics and forecasting. Your CS ops person should still own health scores and renewal workflows.

But they should all write to the same data model. Same object definitions. Same lifecycle stages. Same source of truth for what a "qualified" anything means.

The unification happens at the schema level, not the org chart level.

I've seen this play out three times now. Teams that reorganized around RevOps as a title spent 6 months figuring out roles. Teams that just standardized their data definitions started seeing cross-funnel insights in weeks.

Org charts are slow. Schemas are fast.
posts.cta: Has anyone actually seen a full RevOps unification work well? Genuinely curious what made it click.

inspired_by.author: Dan Khan
inspired_by.summary: Outlines how revenue operations can unify sales ops, marketing ops, and customer success through data orchestration. Highlights tools for outbound automation and the shift to GTM engineering mindsets. Provides case study on 3x pipeline growth via integrated systems.

remixes.label: contrarian-flip
remixes.hook: RevOps broke the thing it was supposed to fix.
remixes.body:
RevOps broke the thing it was supposed to fix.

The promise was simple. Unify sales ops, marketing ops, and CS ops. One team, one data model, no more finger pointing between departments.

What actually happened at most companies? They took three specialists who deeply understood their domains and made them generalists who understand nothing deeply.

The sales ops person who knew every Salesforce workflow, every rep's process quirks, every pipeline stage nuance. Now they're maintaining marketing automation too. Badly.

The marketing ops person who could build complex nurture sequences in their sleep. Now they're debugging sales commission calculations. Also badly.

You didn't get alignment. You got dilution.

The better model I've seen work is what I'd call "federated ops." Each ops person stays deep in their domain. But they share a common data layer and meet weekly to align on definitions, handoff criteria, and shared metrics.

The difference is subtle but critical. Federated ops preserves domain expertise while solving the alignment problem. Full RevOps unification solves the alignment problem by destroying domain expertise.

The companies that tried full unification and walked it back all say the same thing. "We lost too much institutional knowledge." You can rebuild processes. Rebuilding the intuition that comes from years in a specific domain takes much longer.
remixes.cta: Has your company tried the unified RevOps model? What actually happened?

remixes.label: systems-design-lens
remixes.hook: RevOps is a systems design problem, and most companies are implementing the wrong architecture.
remixes.body:
RevOps is a systems design problem, and most companies are implementing the wrong architecture.

If you're a developer, think of it this way. The RevOps pitch is basically a monolith. Take three separate services (sales ops, marketing ops, CS ops), merge them into one service, and hope it's simpler to maintain.

We already know how that plays out in software. Monoliths work great when they're small. Then they grow, and suddenly every change risks breaking something in a completely different domain.

The same thing happens with RevOps teams. At 1-3 people, a unified team works fine. Everyone knows everything. Changes are easy to coordinate. But as complexity grows, that single team becomes a bottleneck. Every request has to go through people who may not understand the domain.

The microservices equivalent for ops? Keep domain-specific teams but enforce shared contracts. Common data definitions. Agreed-upon handoff points. Shared metrics dashboards. Automated data sync between tools.

You don't need one team. You need one data model that all teams commit to. The org chart doesn't create alignment. The shared standards do.

I've seen this work at companies from 50 to 500 people. Keep the specialists. Invest in the integration layer. That's where the actual RevOps value lives.
remixes.cta: Monolith or microservices: which architecture does your ops team run on?

posts.title: Content strategy without outbound is a blog
posts.hook: Content strategy without distribution is just a blog nobody reads.
posts.body:
Content strategy without distribution is just a blog nobody reads.

I keep seeing teams invest heavily in content. Hire writers, build editorial calendars, produce weekly thought leadership. Then they publish it on their website and hope SEO does the work.

SEO takes months. Your pipeline review is next Tuesday.

The play that actually works is pairing content with outbound triggers.

Real example from my own pipeline:

1. I publish a tactical post about a specific workflow (Clay enrichment, CRM automation, whatever)
2. A script monitors engagement signals... who comments, who shares, who visits the linked resource
3. Those signals feed into an enrichment step. Company, title, tech stack, recent activity
4. Qualified signals get routed to a personalized outbound sequence that references the specific content they engaged with

The content isn't the strategy. The content is the signal generator.

Most teams treat content and outbound as separate functions. Content team publishes. Outbound team prospects. They never talk to each other.

When you wire them together, your content does double duty. It builds authority AND generates warm outbound leads. The prospect already engaged with your thinking before you ever reach out.

That's not content marketing. That's content-led GTM.
posts.cta: How do you connect your content to your outbound pipeline? Or are they still running in parallel?

inspired_by.author: Sarah Chen
inspired_by.summary: Explains content strategy as the backbone of modern GTM, especially when paired with outbound automation for lead nurturing. Details how sales ops teams can repurpose content into personalized sequences. Shares metrics showing 40% uplift in response rates.

remixes.label: distribution-first-flip
remixes.hook: Your content team produces 4 blog posts a month. Your sales team sends 200 emails a week. They've never coordinated once.
remixes.body:
Your content team produces 4 blog posts a month. Your sales team sends 200 emails a week. They've never coordinated once.

This is the default state at most B2B companies. Marketing writes thought leadership. Sales writes cold outreach. They share a Slack channel and nothing else.

The fix isn't better content. It's better wiring.

When a prospect engages with your outbound sequence, your content should show up in their feed within 48 hours. When someone reads three blog posts, your SDR should know about it before their next call block.

Here's what that looks like in practice. Your CRM tracks content engagement by account. When a target account hits two or more page views, it triggers a signal to your outbound tool. The rep gets a notification with the specific pages visited and a suggested talk track.

Now your $4,000/month content investment is feeding your pipeline instead of feeding Google's index.

Most teams treat content and outbound as parallel programs. The ones closing deals treat them as one system with two channels. The content warms the account. The outbound converts it. Neither works as well alone.

The question isn't whether your content is good enough. It's whether anyone with a quota ever sees it.
remixes.cta: When was the last time your content team and sales team sat in the same pipeline review?

remixes.label: anti-seo-rant
remixes.hook: SEO is a 6-month bet. Your quota resets in 30 days. Something has to give.
remixes.body:
SEO is a 6-month bet. Your quota resets in 30 days. Something has to give.

I'm not anti-SEO. I run keyword tracking daily. But I've watched too many early-stage teams pour resources into organic content strategies that won't pay off until Q3 while their pipeline is empty right now.

The move that changed my results was treating every piece of content as outbound ammunition first and SEO collateral second.

Write the post. Publish it. Then immediately use it. Drop the link in a cold email as a relevant resource. Share it in a LinkedIn DM when a prospect asks a question you just answered. Attach it to a follow-up sequence as "something I wrote that might help."

One article I wrote about automation ROI got 200 organic views in its first month. Decent. But I also sent it directly to 40 prospects who had mentioned automation in their job postings. Three replied. One became a client.

The 200 views generated zero pipeline. The 40 sends generated real revenue.

Content without distribution is a hobby. Content paired with intentional outreach is a pipeline engine. The difference isn't the writing. It's what happens after you hit publish.
remixes.cta: Next time you publish something, send it to 10 prospects before you check your analytics. See what happens.

posts.title: AI in CI/CD is underrated
posts.hook: The most underrated AI use case in dev tooling isn't code completion. It's CI/CD.
posts.body:
The most underrated AI use case in dev tooling isn't code completion. It's CI/CD.

Everyone's focused on Copilot and code generation. Those are fine. But the real time sink for solo builders isn't writing code. It's the deploy cycle.

Build breaks. Environment mismatches. Dependency conflicts. Flaky tests. All the stuff that eats 45 minutes before you even know if your code works.

I've been running a setup where an AI agent handles pre-deploy checks:

➡️ Scans for security issues (exposed keys, missing .gitignore entries)
➡️ Validates build before pushing
➡️ Checks for blocklist violations in commit content
➡️ Runs the full test suite and surfaces failures with context

It's not glamorous. There's no "watch AI write a whole app" demo. But it catches the stuff I'd miss at 11pm when I just want to ship.

Last week it caught a partner name that would have been pushed to a public repo. That one save paid for the entire setup.

AI code completion helps you write code faster. AI in CI/CD helps you ship code safer. For solo builders with no code review team, the second one matters more.
posts.cta: What's in your pre-push safety net? Or are you still yolo-pushing to main?

inspired_by.author: Guillermo Rauch
inspired_by.summary: Announces new developer tools integrating AI agents into Next.js workflows for seamless automation of deployment and CI/CD pipelines. Demonstrates how these tools reduce boilerplate code and accelerate prototyping. Calls for community feedback on agent extensibility.

remixes.label: deploy-tax-breakdown
remixes.hook: I tracked my time for a week. 38% went to deploy cycles, not writing code.
remixes.body:
I tracked my time for a week. 38% went to deploy cycles, not writing code.

Build breaks. Environment drift. Dependency conflicts. Flaky tests that pass locally and fail in CI. Debugging why staging doesn't match production.

This is the actual tax on shipping software. And it's where AI delivers the highest ROI for solo builders and small teams.

Not code generation. Deploy intelligence.

Here's what I run now. A pre-push hook powered by an AI agent scans for common failure patterns before code leaves my machine. It checks dependency compatibility, runs a targeted subset of tests based on what changed, and flags environment mismatches.

A second agent monitors build logs and surfaces the actual root cause when something breaks, not the 400-line stack trace, but the one line that matters.

A third handles rollback decisions. If a deploy fails health checks, it reverts automatically and writes a summary of what went wrong.

Before this setup, a failed deploy could eat 45 minutes of context switching. Now most failures resolve without me touching anything.

The irony is that everyone's building AI into their editors while the deploy pipeline, where developers lose the most time, stays manual. The highest-leverage AI investment for most teams isn't in the IDE. It's in everything that happens after you write the code.
remixes.cta: Where do you lose the most time in your dev workflow? Writing code or shipping it?

remixes.label: copilot-misdirection
remixes.hook: Copilot saves you 10 minutes writing a function. A broken deploy costs you 2 hours. Why is all the AI investment going to the wrong problem?
remixes.body:
Copilot saves you 10 minutes writing a function. A broken deploy costs you 2 hours. Why is all the AI investment going to the wrong problem?

The developer tools market is obsessed with code generation. Tab-complete your way to shipping faster. But if you actually measure where time goes for a solo developer or small team, writing code is maybe 30% of the work.

The other 70% is testing, deploying, debugging infrastructure, and recovering from failures. That's where the real bottleneck lives.

I started treating my CI/CD pipeline as an AI-first system six months ago. The results surprised me.

Pre-push validation catches 80% of issues before they hit CI. Build failure analysis reduces debugging time from 30 minutes to 3. Automated rollback on health check failures means broken deploys fix themselves at 3am instead of waking me up.

The compound effect is massive. I ship more frequently because the cost of shipping went down. When deploys are cheap and safe, you push smaller changes more often. Smaller changes are easier to debug. Easier debugging means faster iteration.

The best AI investment for most developers isn't a smarter autocomplete. It's a smarter deploy pipeline that catches problems before users see them.
remixes.cta: What's your deploy failure rate? And how long does recovery take when something breaks?

The implementation I recommend starts with three layers. First, pre-push validation. An AI agent that runs before code leaves your machine, catching dependency conflicts and common failure patterns. Second, intelligent build analysis. When CI fails, surface the root cause in plain language instead of making developers parse 400-line logs. Third, automated recovery. If a deploy fails health checks, roll back automatically and generate a post-mortem.

The compound effect matters most. When deploys become cheap and safe, teams ship smaller changes more frequently. Smaller changes are easier to review, easier to debug, and easier to roll back. This creates a positive feedback loop that improves velocity across the board.

My recommendation: measure your mean time to recovery on failed deploys this week. That number tells you how much value AI-powered CI/CD would deliver for your team.

posts.title: Career pivots are oversimplified
posts.hook:
"I quit my corporate job and built a SaaS that does $10k/mo."

Cool. Nobody talks about months 2 through 11.
posts.body:
"I quit my corporate job and built a SaaS that does $10k/mo."

Cool. Nobody talks about months 2 through 11.

The career pivot narrative on LinkedIn is compressed into a before/after. Corporate drone becomes indie hacker. Miserable employee becomes free builder. $0 to $10k MRR in 30 days.

The messy middle gets edited out.

Month 2: your product works but nobody cares.
Month 4: you pivot for the first time. It feels like failure.
Month 7: you're burning savings and questioning the whole thing.
Month 9: something clicks but revenue is still $200/mo.
Month 11: you either figure out distribution or you go back.

I didn't pivot from corporate to indie overnight. I automated my way out gradually. Kept the job. Built tools on the side. Let the tools prove themselves before making them the whole thing.

The pivot stories that actually help people aren't "I jumped and the net appeared." They're "I built the net while I was still on the ledge."

Practical version:

✅ Automate one part of your current job with tools you want to learn
✅ Document what you build (this becomes your portfolio)
✅ Ship something small that solves a real problem you have
✅ Let traction decide when to go full-time, not motivation

Motivation is a terrible business signal. Revenue is a good one.
posts.cta: For those who've pivoted: what did months 2-11 actually look like?

inspired_by.author: Sahil Lavingia
inspired_by.summary: Sahil reflects on laying off his entire team to go solo, pivoting Gumroad to profitability at $10M+ ARR. He discusses the power of building lean as a founder and shipping continuously in public. Post includes revenue graphs and advice for tech career pivots to entrepreneurship.

remixes.label: messy-middle-timeline
remixes.hook: Month 1 after leaving my corporate job: pure adrenaline. Month 4: refreshing Stripe at 2am hoping for a $29 charge.
remixes.body:
Month 1 after leaving my corporate job: pure adrenaline. Month 4: refreshing Stripe at 2am hoping for a $29 charge.

The career pivot posts on LinkedIn skip the boring, painful, humbling part. The part where you build something and nobody cares. Where you realize your corporate network doesn't convert to customers. Where you question the decision every single week.

Here's what my timeline actually looked like.

Month 1: Built the MVP. Felt unstoppable. Told everyone I knew.
Month 2: Launched to crickets. 12 signups, 0 paying.
Month 3: Rebuilt the landing page twice. Started cold outreach. Got ignored.
Month 4: First paying customer. $29/month. Felt like a million dollars.
Month 5: Customer churned. Back to zero MRR.
Month 6: Ran out of savings buffer. Took a part-time contract to stay alive.
Month 7: Hated the contract work. Resented it for eating into product time.
Month 8: Finally found a repeatable channel. Small but real.
Month 9-11: Slow grind. $200/month. Then $800. Then $2,400.

That's 11 months of ugly, nonlinear, unglamorous work compressed into a LinkedIn post that says "I quit my job and now I make $10k/month."

The pivot worked. But not because of some revelation or framework. It worked because I didn't quit during the months that sucked.
remixes.cta: If you're in months 2-11 right now, what's keeping you going? Genuinely want to know.

remixes.label: survivorship-bias-call
remixes.hook: For every "I quit and built a $10k MRR SaaS" post, there are 50 people who quit, built something, and went back to a job. They just don't post about it.
remixes.body:
For every "I quit and built a $10k MRR SaaS" post, there are 50 people who quit, built something, and went back to a job. They just don't post about it.

This isn't a failure narrative. Going back to employment after trying something is a perfectly valid outcome. But LinkedIn's algorithm rewards the hero arc, not the honest one.

The survivorship bias in career pivot content is extreme. You see the winners because they have something to sell. The course. The coaching program. The "how I did it" thread.

What you don't see is the founder who burned through 8 months of savings, built a product that solved a real problem, and still couldn't find customers. Not because they were bad at building. Because distribution is a different skill than engineering, and most technical founders learn that the hard way.

Or the person who pivoted, made it work for 18 months, and then chose to go back to a salaried role because they missed health insurance and not thinking about payroll at 11pm.

Both of those outcomes are fine. But pretending they don't exist creates a distorted picture that pressures people into decisions based on incomplete information.

If you're considering a pivot, talk to people who tried and came back. Not just the ones who made it. Their perspective is more useful than any success story.
remixes.cta: Has anyone here tried the indie route and intentionally gone back to employment? I'd love to hear what drove that decision.

"I quit my corporate job and built a SaaS that does $10k/mo."

Here's what I tell anyone considering a career pivot: plan for the messy middle, not the highlight reel.

The career pivot narrative on LinkedIn compresses a painful, nonlinear journey into a before/after. Corporate drone becomes indie hacker. $0 to $10k MRR in 30 days. The editing removes the months that actually determine whether you survive.

Based on what I've seen and experienced, here's the realistic framework.

Months 1-2: Adrenaline carries you. You ship fast. You feel free.
Months 3-5: Reality hits. Your product works but nobody cares. Your network doesn't convert to customers. Cold outreach gets ignored.
Months 6-8: Financial pressure builds. You take contract work to buy time. You resent it.
Months 9-11: If you're still alive, things start compounding. Slowly. Painfully slowly.

My recommendation to anyone in this position: have 12 months of runway, not 6. Expect your first 5 months of revenue to be embarrassingly small. And build your distribution channel before you need it, not after you launch.

The pivot can absolutely work. But it works because of persistence through the ugly months, not because of some magical framework.

The actual story? Month 2, your product works and nobody cares. Month 4, you pivot for the first time and realize pivoting means throwing away weeks of work. Month 6, you take a contract gig because your savings are bleeding out. Month 8, you question everything.

The worst part? This edited narrative actively hurts people. Someone reads your compressed success story, quits their job with 3 months of savings, and flames out because they expected the timeline you advertised.

If you've actually pivoted, share the real version. The version with the months that sucked. That's the post people actually need.

posts.title: Stop building general purpose agents
posts.hook: Every failed AI agent project I've seen started with the same sentence: "It should be able to handle anything."
posts.body:
Every failed AI agent project I've seen started with the same sentence: "It should be able to handle anything."

General-purpose agents are a trap. They demo well. They ship never.

The pattern that works is building single-purpose agents and composing them. Each agent does one thing, does it reliably, and has clear inputs and outputs.

My production setup right now:

🔧 Agent 1: Scans Reddit/X for relevant conversations. Output: scored queue.
🔧 Agent 2: Enriches company data from multiple sources. Output: enriched record.
🔧 Agent 3: Generates content drafts from templates. Output: draft for human review.
🔧 Agent 4: Monitors SEO rankings and logs changes. Output: daily brief.

None of these agents talk to each other directly. They write to shared data stores. Another process reads those stores and decides what happens next.

This is boring. It's also the only architecture I've seen work reliably past the demo stage.

The teams building one mega-agent that does research AND writes AND deploys AND monitors? They're still debugging prompt chains. The teams with five dumb agents and a scheduler? They shipped last month.

Composition over capability. Every time.
posts.cta: What's your agent architecture look like? One big brain or many small ones?

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Analyzes the AI infrastructure stack powering agentic AI, from vector databases to orchestration layers. Highlights open-source tools like Ray and CrewAI for building scalable automation systems. Discusses investment trends in AI infra startups.

remixes.label: lego-architecture
remixes.hook: I stopped building smart AI agents and started building dumb ones. Revenue went up.
remixes.body:
I stopped building smart AI agents and started building dumb ones. Revenue went up.

Sounds backwards. But the smartest architecture I've shipped is a collection of agents that are each individually stupid.

Agent 1 reads RSS feeds and outputs a JSON list of URLs. That's it. It doesn't analyze. It doesn't summarize. It reads and outputs.

Agent 2 takes a URL and extracts the key facts. One URL in, one summary out. No decisions.

Agent 3 takes a summary and decides if it's relevant to a specific topic. Yes or no. Binary.

Agent 4 drafts a response using a template and the relevant summaries. Fill in the blanks.

Each one is trivial to test. Each one fails in predictable ways. Each one can be replaced without touching the others.

Compare this to the "general purpose agent" approach where one mega-agent handles the entire workflow. It reads feeds, decides relevance, drafts responses, and manages its own context. When it fails, and it will fail, you have no idea which part broke.

The Lego approach isn't glamorous. It doesn't demo well at conferences. But it ships. It runs reliably at 3am when nobody's watching. And when something breaks, you fix one small piece instead of debugging an entire reasoning chain.

Intelligence isn't in the individual agent. It's in the composition.
remixes.cta: What's the simplest agent you've built that actually runs in production? Sometimes boring is best.

remixes.label: agent-graveyard-lessons
remixes.hook: I've killed 4 AI agent projects this year. Every one died from the same disease: scope.
remixes.body:
I've killed 4 AI agent projects this year. Every one died from the same disease: scope.

Project 1: "Build an agent that handles all customer support." Dead in 3 weeks. Too many edge cases. Couldn't handle the long tail of weird requests.

Project 2: "Build an agent that manages our entire content pipeline." Dead in 2 weeks. The pipeline had 14 steps and the agent couldn't maintain coherent decision-making across all of them.

Project 3: "Build an agent that does research and then acts on the research." Dead in a month. The research quality was fine. The acting-on-it quality was terrible. Mixing reasoning and execution in one agent is a recipe for hallucinated actions.

Project 4: "Build an intelligent assistant that learns from every interaction." Dead on arrival. The state management alone was a full engineering project.

What survived? Single-purpose agents with clear boundaries.

A Reddit scanner that finds relevant threads. That's all it does.
An enrichment agent that takes a company name and returns structured data. One input, one output.
A draft generator that fills in a template. No creativity, just assembly.

Each one runs daily in production. Each one has been running for months without breaking. The boring, constrained agents outlived every ambitious one.

The lesson isn't that AI agents don't work. It's that the word "agent" tricks you into thinking bigger than you should.
remixes.cta: What's in your AI agent graveyard? What scope killed it?

tones.advisor:
Every failed AI agent project I've seen started with the same sentence: "It should be able to handle anything."

Here's my recommendation based on building and shipping agents in production: constrain aggressively.

General-purpose agents demo well and ship never. The pattern that consistently works is building single-purpose agents and composing them. Each agent handles one task, produces one output type, and fails in predictable ways.

The architecture I recommend has three principles. First, one agent, one job. A scanning agent scans. An enrichment agent enriches. A drafting agent drafts. No agent makes decisions outside its domain. Second, clear interfaces. Every agent takes structured input and produces structured output. JSON in, JSON out. Third, independent failure. When one agent breaks, the others keep running. You fix one piece instead of debugging an entire chain.

In practice, this means your "intelligent pipeline" is actually five or six dumb steps connected by a simple orchestrator. Each step is trivial to test, trivial to replace, and trivial to monitor.

The compound benefit is reliability. Single-purpose agents run at 3am without supervision. General-purpose agents need babysitting. For production systems, predictability beats intelligence every time.

My advice: if your agent description contains the word "and" more than twice, split it.
tones.provocateur:
Every failed AI agent project I've seen started with the same sentence: "It should be able to handle anything."

And yet every week there's a new Twitter thread about some "autonomous agent framework" that's going to replace entire teams. The demo video looks incredible. 47 stars on GitHub by Tuesday. Abandoned by Friday.

General-purpose agents are the cold fusion of software engineering. Always 6 months away from working. Perpetually impressive in controlled demos. Completely useless in production.

You know what actually runs in production? Boring agents. Embarrassingly simple agents. Agents that do one thing so narrowly that calling them "agents" feels generous.

My Reddit scanner doesn't reason. It pattern-matches against a keyword list and outputs URLs. My enrichment pipeline doesn't think. It calls three APIs and merges the results. My content drafter doesn't create. It fills in a template.

None of these would get upvotes on Hacker News. All of them have been running daily for months without breaking.

The AI agent hype is building a generation of engineers who think the goal is building something smart. The goal is building something that works at 3am when nobody's watching. Those are very different things.

Stop trying to build Jarvis. Build five tiny scripts that each do one thing. You'll ship more this month than most agent startups ship all year.

posts.title: The $8/day AI team
posts.hook: My AI costs $8/day. It replaced work that used to take a junior hire 20 hours a week.
posts.body:
My AI costs $8/day. It replaced work that used to take a junior hire 20 hours a week.

Not a flex. Just math.

Breakdown of what runs daily:

➡️ Lead enrichment pipeline: ~$2/day in API calls
➡️ Content drafting and research: ~$3/day
➡️ SEO monitoring and keyword tracking: ~$1/day
➡️ Social listening and engagement scouting: ~$2/day

Total: roughly $240/month.

A part-time contractor doing the same work? $2,000-3,000/month minimum. And they'd need onboarding, management, and wouldn't run at 2am.

But here's what the cost comparison misses.

The AI doesn't replace the human judgment. I still review every outbound message. I still edit every content draft. I still decide which leads to pursue.

What it replaces is the gathering. The enriching. The formatting. The scheduling. The monitoring. All the work that has to happen before a human can make a good decision.

The ROI isn't "AI is cheaper than people." The ROI is "I can operate at a scale that would normally require a team, while keeping quality control in one person's hands."

Solo operators who figure out this math have a structural advantage that's hard to compete with.
posts.cta: Track your AI spend for a week. Compare it to what you'd pay a human. The math is wild.

remixes.label: time-audit-angle
remixes.hook: I used to spend 20 hours a week on tasks that required zero judgment. Now a machine does them while I sleep.
remixes.body:
I used to spend 20 hours a week on tasks that required zero judgment. Now a machine does them while I sleep.

Not all work requires a human brain. Some of it is pure process. Look up this company. Pull these data points. Check if this keyword ranked. Find relevant conversations on social media. Draft a first pass.

The common thread? These tasks follow a pattern. They have clear inputs and outputs. And the cost of a mediocre result is low because a human reviews the output anyway.

I started by listing every recurring task in my week and asking one question: does this require judgment, or does it require following steps?

The steps-only list was longer than I expected.

Lead enrichment: pull company data from three sources, merge into a profile. No judgment needed. Now automated.

Keyword tracking: check rankings for 40 keywords daily, flag changes. No judgment needed. Now automated.

Social listening: scan Reddit and X for conversations matching my topics. No judgment needed. Now automated.

Content research: gather sources and pull relevant quotes on a topic. Low judgment needed. Now partially automated.

The total cost for all of this? Roughly $8/day in API calls and compute. The total time saved? About 20 hours a week of tasks that were eating my schedule but not using my brain.

The ROI isn't theoretical. It's hours back in your week, doing work that actually requires you.
remixes.cta: List your recurring weekly tasks. How many of them actually require your judgment versus just following steps?

remixes.label: hire-vs-automate-math
remixes.hook: A part-time contractor costs $2,500/month. My automation stack costs $240. They do the same work. One of them never calls in sick.
remixes.body:
A part-time contractor costs $2,500/month. My automation stack costs $240. They do the same work. One of them never calls in sick.

I'm not making a philosophical argument here. This is a spreadsheet exercise.

Four tasks run daily in my business. Lead enrichment, content research and drafting, SEO monitoring, and social listening. Before automation, these required roughly 20 hours of human effort per week. At contractor rates, that's $2,500/month minimum for someone competent.

The automated version uses API calls to various services, a local language model for low-stakes processing, and cloud models for tasks requiring more reasoning. Total monthly spend: $240.

But here's the part that matters more than cost. The automated version runs at midnight. Every night. Without reminders, without status updates, without scope discussions. When I wake up, the results are in a dashboard.

The contractor version required me to write briefs, review work, give feedback, and manage the relationship. That management overhead, maybe 3 hours a week, never shows up in the cost comparison but it's real.

I want to be clear: this doesn't replace senior thinking. It replaces process work. The kind of work where you're following steps, not making decisions. A human still reviews everything before it goes out.

The question isn't whether AI can replace your team. It's whether you have tasks that don't require a team in the first place.
remixes.cta: What process work are you still doing manually that follows a repeatable pattern? That's your automation candidate.

Not a flex. Just math. And here's how I'd recommend thinking about it for your own operation.

The key principle is identifying process work versus judgment work. Process work follows repeatable steps with clear inputs and outputs. Judgment work requires expertise, context, and human decision-making. Automate the former. Protect the latter.

Here's my current breakdown. Lead enrichment pipeline runs daily at roughly $2 in API calls. Content drafting and research costs about $3/day using a mix of local and cloud models. SEO monitoring and keyword tracking is $1/day. Social listening and engagement scouting is $2/day. Total: approximately $240/month.

A part-time contractor doing equivalent work costs $2,500/month minimum, plus management overhead that adds another 3 hours of your time weekly.

My recommendation for anyone considering this: start with one task. Pick the most repetitive, lowest-judgment item on your weekly list. Automate it. Measure the time savings. Then expand.

Important caveat: the automated output still gets human review before anything goes live. This isn't about removing humans from the process. It's about removing humans from the parts of the process that don't need them.

Track your AI spend for one week. Compare it to what you'd pay a human for the same output. The numbers speak for themselves.
tones.provocateur:
My AI costs $8/day. It replaced work that used to take a junior hire 20 hours a week.

Not a flex. Just math. Math that a lot of people in this industry don't want to talk about because the implications are uncomfortable.

Let me be direct. If your job consists primarily of following steps, pulling data from one place and putting it in another, checking dashboards, compiling reports, drafting first passes of content. Your job is an API call waiting to happen.

I run four automated pipelines daily. Lead enrichment, content research, SEO monitoring, social listening. Total cost: $240/month. A contractor doing the same work costs ten times that and needs managing.

The managing part is what people forget. A contractor requires briefs, check-ins, feedback cycles, and scope discussions. That overhead eats 3 hours a week minimum. The automation requires zero management. It runs at midnight and delivers results by morning.

But here's nobody posts about: this math applies to a lot of roles that companies are still hiring for. Not senior roles. Not roles requiring deep expertise. But the entry-level, process-heavy roles that people depend on as stepping stones.

I don't have a clean answer for what that means at scale. But pretending the math doesn't exist isn't an answer either.

The $8/day AI team isn't coming. It's already here.

platforms.linkedin:
My AI costs $8/day. It replaced work that used to take a junior hire 20 hours a week.

Not a flex. Just math.

Breakdown of what runs daily:

➡️ Lead enrichment pipeline: ~$2/day in API calls
➡️ Content drafting and research: ~$3/day
➡️ SEO monitoring and keyword tracking: ~$1/day
➡️ Social listening and engagement scouting: ~$2/day

Total: roughly $240/month.

A part-time contractor doing the same work? $2,000-3,000/month minimum. And they'd need onboarding, management, and wouldn't run at 2am.

But here's what the cost comparison misses.

The AI doesn't replace the human judgment. I still review every outbound message. I still edit every content draft. I still decide which leads to pursue.

What it replaces is the gathering. The enriching. The formatting. The scheduling. The monitoring. All the work that has to happen before a human can make a good decision.

The ROI isn't "AI is cheaper than people." The ROI is "I can operate at a scale that would normally require a team, while keeping quality control in one person's hands."

Solo operators who figure out this math have a structural advantage that's hard to compete with.
